parentQuestion: Nerve fibers from the medial aspect of each eye cross over to the opposite side at the chiasma.

scaffolds.q: The optic CHIASMA is where the two optic nerves meet.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The chiasma is the X-shaped junction where optic nerves meet and partially cross.

scaffolds.q: At the chiasma, SOME fibers cross to the opposite side while others don't.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! It's a PARTIAL decussation, not complete crossing.

scaffolds.q: The NASAL retina (medial half) sees the TEMPORAL visual field (lateral side).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Light from the temporal (outer) field hits the nasal (inner) retina.

scaffolds.q: NASAL retinal fibers CROSS at the chiasma to the opposite side.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Nasal = medial = crosses. This reorganizes from 'per eye' to 'per visual field.'

scaffolds.q: TEMPORAL retinal fibers stay on the SAME side (don't cross).
After the chiasma, each optic TRACT carries information from ONE visual hemifield.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Left tract = right visual field (from both eyes). Right tract = left visual field.

scaffolds.q: This arrangement allows binocular processing of each visual field by one hemisphere.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Essential for depth perception and unified visual experience.

scaffolds.q: Medial (nasal) retinal fibers cross at the chiasma; temporal fibers don't.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Partial decussation reorganizes visual information by visual field, not by eye.

scaffolds.q: The answer is TRUE - nasal (medial) retinal fibers cross at the chiasma.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! This partial crossing ensures each hemisphere receives input from both eyes but for only its contralateral visual field.
